_comment: Every constant that steers behaviour is here, classified, and the test
directions -- that this file and the code agree. A constant cannot drift from
its receipt, and a new constant cannot be added without landing here.
tuned  = derived from measurement. A different measurement would have given a
different value. Owes EVIDENCE: a committed file, the key inside it
that IS the value, and the rule by which the evidence chose it.
policy = a deliberate decision about behaviour, not an empirical optimum. A cap
of 64 attribute keys is not the measured best number of attribute
keys; it is a statement about what we are willing to accept. Owes a
RATIONALE, pointed at prose, which is reviewable.
The distinction exists to be abuse-proof: without it, every inconvenient constant
gets reclassified as policy to escape the evidence requirement.
Charter C-3: a `tuned` entry also records the golden-corpus version it was derived
against, declares its policy bounds with a rationale for why measurement cannot see
them, and is marked corpus_conditional when derived on a hash-embedder corpus.
corpus_conditional means: we already know this value may move. Issue #3 tracks
re-deriving every one of them on a real-embedding corpus.

constants.name: DEFAULT_NPROBE
constants.rule: the smallest probe count whose p1 recall@10 clears 0.8 on the golden corpus. Chosen on the TAIL, not the mean: at nprobe=1 the mean is 0.941 while two queries return nothing at all. Rose from 4 to 6 in S5 -- not because the engine got worse, but because the codebook stopped depending on a lucky input order (charter C-4, D-034/D-036). The old value was measured against a codebook that a layout accident had flattered.
constants.corpus_conditional_reason: derived on the deterministic hash-embedder corpus, whose motifs are unusually well-separated. On a real embedding space this value may move -- issue #3.

policy_bounds.bound: chosen on the p1 tail, not the mean
policy_bounds.why_measurement_cannot_see_it: A mean is not a policy. At nprobe=1 the mean recall is 0.904 while five queries return NOTHING AT ALL. Optimising the mean is how a system with total failures in it gets shipped as '90% accurate'.

constants.generation_conditional_reason: A new codebook generation changes the PQ geometry, so this constant may not transfer across one. Re-sweep on a new generation -- the same standing obligation as re-sweeping on a real-embedding corpus (charter C-3, issue #3).

constants.name: BLOCK_SIZE
constants.rule: the block size minimising physically-read bytes across the golden query set (whole blocks, frame headers and over-read, measured by the reader), subject to the block DIRECTORY staying under 4 bytes/row. The budget is on the directory term ALONE, isolated by subtracting the fixed manifest overhead (the S4/S5 per-tenant-stats and lineage extensions, which do not scale with block size) -- before S6 the manifest was small enough that whole-manifest was a fine proxy, but the extensions broke that. Re-derived from 4096 to 2048 in S6 when the sweep was re-run against the current engine (determinism contract §5): the corrected directory budget makes 2048 eligible, and it reads fewer bytes than 4096.
constants.corpus_conditional_reason: derived on the deterministic hash-embedder corpus, whose motifs are unusually well-separated. On a real embedding space this value may move -- issue #3.

policy_bounds.why_measurement_cannot_see_it: The directory is read IN FULL on every part open, including the opens that then prune the part away without reading a column. At a 2,000-row corpus that term is invisible, so the byte-count objective collapses onto the smallest candidate -- which at 512-byte blocks would give a billion-row part a ~16 GB directory that every reader must load before it can decide the part is irrelevant. No number of saved read-bytes buys that back.

constants.generation_conditional_reason: A new codebook generation changes the PQ geometry, so this constant may not transfer across one. Re-sweep on a new generation -- the same standing obligation as re-sweeping on a real-embedding corpus (charter C-3, issue #3).

constants.name: DEFAULT_CANDIDATES
constants.rule: swept JOINTLY with DEFAULT_RERANK against the frozen golden corpus, holding nprobe at its own receipted value. They interact -- the candidate width decides who is allowed to be reranked, the rerank width decides how many of them actually are -- so an independent single-axis sweep of either measures a cross-section of a surface and reports it as the surface. Among the points clearing both tail floors (p1 recall@10 >= 0.8 AND zero_recall_queries == 0, the same floors nprobe was chosen against), smallest rerank first, then smallest candidates.
constants.corpus_conditional_reason: derived on the deterministic hash-embedder corpus, whose motifs are unusually well-separated. On a real embedding space this value may move -- issue #3.

policy_bounds.why_measurement_cannot_see_it: A rerank budget wider than the candidate list is not a configuration, it is a misunderstanding: you cannot exactly score rows that never entered the heap. Points where rerank > candidates are really just 'rerank = candidates' and would pad the receipt.

constants.generation_conditional_reason: A new codebook generation changes the PQ geometry, so this constant may not transfer across one. Re-sweep on a new generation -- the same standing obligation as re-sweeping on a real-embedding corpus (charter C-3, issue #3).

constants.name: DEFAULT_RERANK
constants.rule: swept JOINTLY with DEFAULT_CANDIDATES; see that entry. THE RECALL FLOORS DO NOT BIND ON THIS CORPUS -- every point in the grid clears them, because PQ's top-10 already contains the true top-10 -- so the binding constraint is MIN_PAGEABLE_ROWS: the paginated result set IS the rerank survivor set, and rerank=10 with a page size of 10 would make the cursor decorative. Rerank leads the ordering because it is the expensive control: an exact vector is ~32x a coded row.
constants.corpus_conditional_reason: derived on the deterministic hash-embedder corpus, whose motifs are unusually well-separated. On a real embedding space this value may move -- issue #3.

policy_bounds.why_measurement_cannot_see_it: The paginated result set IS the rerank survivor set (docs/QUERY-CONTRACT.md §4). A rerank width of 10 with a default page size of 10 makes the first page the entire result and the cursor decorative. Recall cannot see this -- and on this corpus the recall floors do not bind at all, so without the bound the sweep chooses rerank=10.

constants.generation_conditional_reason: A new codebook generation changes the PQ geometry, so this constant may not transfer across one. Re-sweep on a new generation -- the same standing obligation as re-sweeping on a real-embedding corpus (charter C-3, issue #3).

constants.name: KMEANS_RESTARTS
constants.rule: the SMALLEST restart count whose codebook reaches the best derived nprobe on the grid (and leaves no query empty). The objective is deliberately NOT inertia -- inertia is what each restart minimizes internally, and best-by-inertia is NOT best-by-recall: at 3 and 5 restarts the lowest-inertia codebook needs SEVEN probes to clear the tail floors, where one unlucky init needed five and eight restarts need four. A tighter fit in the k-means sense can be a more unbalanced one in the IVF sense, and IVF is what a query pays for. So the objective is probes -- which are scan fraction, which is the bill. Smallest-on-the-plateau, because training cost is linear in this and paying for 25 restarts to get what 8 gives you is paying for nothing.
constants.corpus_conditional_reason: derived on the deterministic hash-embedder corpus -- issue #3.
constants.generation_conditional_reason: A new codebook generation changes the PQ geometry, so this constant may not transfer across one. Re-sweep on a new generation -- the same standing obligation as re-sweeping on a real-embedding corpus (charter C-3, issue #3).

policy_bounds.bound: the grid is bounded at 25 restarts
policy_bounds.why_measurement_cannot_see_it: Training cost is linear in this constant and training is on the critical path of a MIGRATION, which is an operation an operator is watching. Measurement will happily keep buying marginal codebook quality with unbounded training time; the bound says how much time we are willing to spend. The chosen value (8) is interior to the grid and sits at the start of a plateau that 12, 16 and 25 do not improve on, so this bound is not what selected it (charter C-3).

constants.note: S5. This constant exists because of charter C-4. When the training sample stopped being keyed on physical position, recall fell below its floor -- k-means++ had been relying on a lucky first point handed to it by the corpus's natural order. The recall we had been reporting was, in part, a coincidence of the input order. Restarts make the codebook depend on the data instead of on a draw.

constants.name: ADAPTIVE_MARGIN_X1000
constants.rule: the LARGEST adaptive margin that (a) stays within a worst-case cost budget at the shipping base -- adaptive probing must not exceed 1.5x the flat probe count -- and (b) demonstrably helps a deliberately STARVED base recover part of its tail, proving the heuristic fires on real boundary queries. On this corpus the recall floor at the shipping base is already met flat, so measurement cannot pick the margin by BENEFIT (every margin gives identical shipping recall); it can only see COST. So a policy cost bound selects the value. The sweep also PROVES monotonicity by refusing any margin that lowers shipping recall, which is what keeps every nprobe/width receipt valid as a floor. v1 is monotone-only: adaptive adds probes, never subtracts.
constants.corpus_conditional_reason: The cluster geometry that decides which queries sit on a boundary is exactly what the deterministic hash embedder cannot represent -- its motifs are unusually well-separated, so almost no query is genuinely on a boundary. issue #3.
constants.generation_conditional_reason: A new codebook generation changes the PQ geometry, so this constant may not transfer across one. Re-sweep on a new generation -- the same standing obligation as re-sweeping on a real-embedding corpus (charter C-3, issue #3).

policy_bounds.bound: adaptive probing at the shipping base must stay within 1.5x the flat probe count
policy_bounds.why_measurement_cannot_see_it: On this corpus the p1 recall floor at the shipping base is ALREADY met flat, so every margin yields identical shipping recall and measurement cannot pick the margin by benefit. It can only see COST, which a larger margin always raises. The real benefit-driven derivation waits for a real-embedding corpus where boundary queries actually miss (issue #3).

constants.note: S6, issue #1 (adaptive nprobe). MONOTONE ONLY in v1: the margin may add probes above DEFAULT_NPROBE for boundary queries and may never go below it, so recall can only improve. Stored x1000 because the ledger holds integers and the margin is 0.05.

constants.name: FP16_COSINE_TOLERANCE_MICROS
constants.rule: the fp16-cosine accuracy contract's tolerance, in micro-units. Must be >= 2x the worst |fp16 - fp32| rerank score gap over every stored vector and every golden query, WITH HEADROOM. The 2x is not arbitrary: if the tolerance exceeds twice the max per-score gap, then two rows whose fp32 scores differ by more than the tolerance have fp16 scores that differ by more than (tolerance - 2*max_gap) > 0 with the same sign, so fp16 can NEVER invert a >tolerance-separated pair. That is exactly the selection-stability the contract promises: fp16 respects the fp32 order except among rows it (by contract) treats as tied. Measured max gap on the golden corpus is ~4.6e-4, so the floor is ~9.2e-4; 2e-3 is the committed value with headroom.
constants.corpus_conditional_reason: The fp16 rounding error depends on the magnitude distribution of the stored vectors, which the hash embedder does not represent faithfully. issue #3.
constants.generation_conditional_reason: A new codebook re-embeds the vectors, changing their magnitudes and thus the fp16 gaps. Re-sweep on a new generation (charter C-6).

policy_bounds.bound: selection stability, not just a score bound
policy_bounds.why_measurement_cannot_see_it: A tolerance that merely bounded score error would still permit fp16 to reorder the answer, which is the thing a lossy encoding must NOT do silently. The binding constraint is that no >tolerance-separated pair inverts -- a property about ORDER, not magnitude -- and it is what makes fp16 a smaller store rather than a different database.

constants.note: S7, D-049. The first negotiated accuracy contract. fp32-exact remains the only default; fp16 is opt-in behind encoding_id=2 / accuracy_contract_id=2, and a build that does not implement the pair REFUSES the part. Contract text, evidence, and the unknown-encoding fixture were all updated in the same change.

constants.name: MAX_ATTRIBUTE_KEYS
constants.rationale: docs/INGESTION-CONTRACT.md §5 -- bounds the size of ONE EVENT, not the shape of the dataset. Sixty-four keys is generous for a real GenAI span (a dozen or so) and small enough that a runaway producer is caught rather than absorbed.

constants.name: MAX_ATTRIBUTE_KEY_BYTES
constants.rationale: docs/INGESTION-CONTRACT.md §5 -- a key is an identifier, not a payload. 128 bytes comfortably fits every OTel GenAI convention key with room to spare, and anything longer is a value that has been mistaken for a key.

constants.name: MAX_ATTRIBUTE_VALUE_BYTES
constants.rationale: docs/INGESTION-CONTRACT.md §5 -- values may be large (a model name, a stop reason, a session id) but they are not documents. Anything past 4 KiB belongs in the body, where it will be embedded and searchable, rather than in a map where it is merely stored.

constants.name: MAX_ATTRIBUTES_BYTES
constants.rationale: docs/INGESTION-CONTRACT.md §5 -- the total budget binds even when every individual value is legal: 32 keys x 4 KiB is 32 legal values adding up to an illegal event.

constants.name: MAX_ATTRIBUTE_KEY_CARDINALITY
constants.rationale: docs/INGESTION-CONTRACT.md §5 -- THE ONE THAT MATTERS. The other caps bound one event; only this bounds the SHAPE of the dataset. A tenant using a uuid as an attribute KEY would otherwise grow a dictionary the size of their traffic, carried in every manifest, forever. That is how a columnar format dies.

constants.name: MAX_LATENESS_MS
constants.rationale: docs/INGESTION-CONTRACT.md §4 -- seven days. Beyond it an event's partition may already have been merged, tiered or expired, and admitting it would resurrect a closed partition.

constants.name: MAX_SKEW_AHEAD_MS
constants.rationale: docs/INGESTION-CONTRACT.md §4 -- one hour. A clock-skewed producer emitting event_time=2099 would make its partition immortal: no retention policy would ever reclaim it.

constants.name: IDEMPOTENCY_WINDOW_MS
constants.rationale: docs/INGESTION-CONTRACT.md §2 -- the honest limit. Beyond this window a replayed key is admitted as a new event and becomes a duplicate ROW, which merge reconciles by last-write-wins on event_time (D-012). Two mechanisms, one seam, documented rather than pretended away.

constants.name: IDEMPOTENCY_MAX_ENTRIES
constants.rationale: docs/INGESTION-CONTRACT.md §2 -- the backstop for a store whose clock never advances. A window that never rolls over is not a bound.

constants.name: MAX_BODY_BYTES
constants.rationale: docs/DECISIONS.md D-005 -- an oversized body is DEAD-LETTERED, never truncated. A stored event whose body is silently half-missing is a lie nobody will ever catch.

constants.name: MAX_EMBED_INPUT_BYTES
constants.rationale: docs/DECISIONS.md D-005 -- embedding cost is bounded; the stored body is not damaged. Distinct from MAX_BODY_BYTES on purpose.

constants.name: MIN_PAGEABLE_ROWS
constants.rationale: docs/QUERY-CONTRACT.md §4 -- the paginated result set IS the plan's rerank survivor set, so the default plan must be able to serve at least five pages at the default page size (5 x 10 = 50). Measurement cannot see this: on the golden corpus the recall floors do not bind at all, and without this bound the joint width sweep chooses rerank=10, making the first page the entire result and the cursor decorative.

constants.name: ADAPTIVE_MAX_NPROBE
constants.rationale: The hard ceiling on adaptive probing (S6, issue #1). A query may never probe more than this many centroids however tight its margins, so a pathological query near many cluster boundaries cannot silently turn an approximate scan into a full one. It bounds WORST-CASE query cost, which measurement of average queries cannot see. Not the measured-best ceiling -- a statement about how much a single query is allowed to cost.

constants.name: MAX_STATEMENT_BYTES
constants.rationale: docs/QUERY-CONTRACT.md §7 -- the SQL text is network-facing input, the same category of thing as a part file. S1's bounded-allocation discipline applies in full.

constants.name: MAX_EXPR_DEPTH
constants.rationale: docs/QUERY-CONTRACT.md §7 -- the one that matters most. A recursive-descent parser without a depth counter is a stack overflow waiting for '((((...))))', and a stack overflow is a PROCESS DEATH, not an error: it cannot be caught, reported, or attributed to the query that caused it.

constants.name: MAX_IN_LIST
constants.rationale: docs/QUERY-CONTRACT.md §7 -- an IN list is an untrusted length that would otherwise reserve capacity on the strength of a number a stranger sent us.

constants.name: MAX_TOKENS
constants.rationale: docs/QUERY-CONTRACT.md §7 -- bounds the lexer independently of the byte cap: 64 KiB of single-character tokens is a different attack from one long string literal.

constants.name: TRAIN_SAMPLE_MAX
constants.rationale: A bound on how long codebook training may take and how much memory it may hold, chosen so a codebook can be trained on a laptop. Not a measured optimum: raising it buys a marginally better-fitted codebook at a linear cost in time, and the recall receipts are what measure whether the fit is good enough. NOTE: this constant steered behaviour from S0 and was never registered -- an existing hole in the ledger, found by the S5 charter-C-4 audit and closed there. The audit was worth running for this alone.

constants.name: STRATUM_FLOOR_SHARE
constants.rationale: docs/GENERATION-CONTRACT.md §2 -- the share (one quarter) of the training sample allocated EQUALLY across partition strata before the remainder goes proportionally. Purely proportional allocation is not stratification: it reproduces exactly the imbalance it claims to fix, because a stratum holding 1% of the rows gets 1% of the sample whether you stratify or not. The floor is what makes a small tenant's geometry visible to the codebook at all. Measurement cannot see this -- a codebook that ignores a quiet tenant still scores well on a corpus dominated by the loud one, which is precisely the problem.

constants.name: BASELINE_CLUSTERS
constants.rationale: docs/GENERATION-CONTRACT.md §7 -- how coarse a summary of 'normal' a drift baseline is. Too few and every cluster is a blur that nothing looks novel against; too many and the baseline memorizes its own window and nothing is ever novel again. S9 owns drift as a feature, and is where this earns a receipt rather than a rationale.

constants.name: BASELINE_QUANTILE_PCT
constants.rationale: The novelty percentile of a baseline's OWN window that becomes its alarm threshold. A threshold picked by hand is a number somebody liked; a threshold calibrated from the data is a statement about the data. At p99 roughly one event in a hundred of normal traffic is 'novel', which is what makes an alarm firing on 20% of a window mean something. An integer on purpose: a constant that escaped C-1 because of its TYPE would be a constant that escaped on a technicality.

constants.name: DRIFT_FIRE_MULTIPLE
constants.rationale: How many times the calibrated novelty rate must be exceeded before a drift alarm fires. The baseline is calibrated so that ~1% of normal traffic is novel; five times the expected rate is not noise, it is a different distribution. Firing at 2x would page somebody every time a tenant deployed a new prompt, and an alarm that pages on deploys is an alarm that gets muted.

constants.name: DIST_COST_MILLI
constants.rationale: The numeraire of the plan cost model (S8): cost is measured in units of one ADC distance, so this is 1.0 (x1000). testing/evidence/cost-model.json measures the absolute distances/sec for documentation; the plan choice needs only the ratio to PRED_COST_MILLI.

constants.name: PRED_COST_MILLI
constants.rationale: The cost of one general-predicate evaluation, relative to a distance (S8). The honest microbench finding (cost-model.json): a real interpreted predicate::eval costs as much as or MORE than a SIMD-batched ADC distance (~2.7x on the dev machine), so the predicate is the expensive per-row op and 'distance-first, predicate-lazily' (semantic-first) tends to win. Committed as a stable in-magnitude value (equal to a distance) rather than an exact ratio -- a constant bound to a stopwatch is a flaky gate. Engine-conditional (C-6): a compiled predicate path would slash it.

constants.name: PLAN_REGRET_BOUND_PCT
constants.rationale: The worst-cell regret bound (S8, directive 3): the chosen plan must be within 15% of the best fixed plan's actual cost in EVERY cell of the selectivity matrix, not on average. An optimizer that wins on average by losing badly in one cell is worse than a fixed heuristic for the customer in that cell. Measurement cannot pick this -- it is a statement of how much regret we are willing to ship. 15% is wide enough for a crude sampled selectivity estimate (directive 7) to meet, tight enough that a plainly-wrong choice fails the gate.

constants.name: MAX_SEMANTIC_K
constants.rationale: docs/QUERY-CONTRACT.md#17 — the ceiling on k for GROUP BY semantic_cluster. A cluster result is read by a human; the exemplars are the product. Past a couple hundred groups it stops being an explanation and becomes a second dataset. A query asking for more is refused with a named limit, never silently clamped, because clamping answers a different question than the one asked. Measurement cannot pick this; it is a statement about what a person can read.

constants.name: SEMANTIC_STATE_BUDGET_BYTES
constants.rationale: docs/QUERY-CONTRACT.md#17 — the clustering working-set budget. A semantic_cluster over a billion-row filtered set must decline, not OOM the node (the S2 lesson, bound the aggregate before it exists). A query whose (rows, dim, k) would exceed 512 MiB is refused with a named limit before the first vector is gathered. 512 MiB admits every corpus S9 clusters and refuses the sets that would need the streaming PQ-code fit filed for the distributed sprint.

constants.name: SEMANTIC_MINIBATCH_SIZE
constants.rationale: docs/DETERMINISM-CONTRACT.md#13 — rows per mini-batch in the k-means fit. A throughput/memory knob, not an optimum: large enough that per-batch overhead is amortized, small enough that the working set stays bounded. Because the fit accumulates in logical (event_id) order regardless of batch size, this changes memory and never the answer.

constants.name: SEMANTIC_MINIBATCH_EPOCHS
constants.rationale: docs/QUERY-CONTRACT.md#15 — passes over the filtered set in the fit, chosen to clear the ARI>=0.8 floor on the frozen labeled corpus with margin. The gate test clustering_recovers_the_true_labels binds it: drop it too low and the ARI floor fails, so the committed test is the receipt. More epochs buy quality with linear cost; this is where the curve flattens on the corpus. Corpus-conditional.

constants.name: SEMANTIC_MINIBATCH_RESTARTS
constants.rationale: docs/DECISIONS.md#d-036 — independent seeded restarts, keeping the lowest-inertia fit. A single k-means++ draw is a lottery (the defect D-036 killed once for the codebook); a single draw scored ARI 0.76 on the corpus and five clears the 0.8 floor. Each restart seed is derived from the content seed, so the choice stays a deterministic function of the data (C-7). The ARI gate binds it. Corpus-conditional.

constants.name: CLUSTER_CONFIDENCE_MIN_X1000
constants.rationale: docs/QUERY-CONTRACT.md#17 — the cluster-quality floor (1 - inertia_k/inertia_1, x1000) below which a result is reported low-confidence. On the frozen corpus the no-structure shape scores ~0.13 and every labeled shape ~0.38-0.44, so the line at 0.25 sits with margin on both sides; the gate test no_structure_is_reported_low_confidence binds it. Corpus-conditional.

constants.name: MERGE_SAFETY_MARGIN_BYTES
constants.rationale: docs/MERGE-CONTRACT.md#3 — free-space headroom required BEYOND the projected merge output before a merge is admitted. A merge writes a second copy before it can free the first, so it is refused with a named deferral unless projected output + this margin fits in free space. 64 MiB is a headroom decision, not a measured optimum: enough that the snapshot commit, WAL, and concurrent ingest have room while a merge runs, small enough not to refuse a merge on a nearly-full disk that the merge would itself relieve. Measurement cannot pick it; it states how close to full we operate.

constants.name: LEASE_TTL_MS
constants.rationale: docs/MERGE-CONTRACT.md#5 — the maximum time a reader may hold a snapshot pinned across a paginated query (invariant 4/6). A query paginating longer than this has its lease expire and its next page returns the explicit expired-snapshot error rather than a stale answer. One hour is generous for an interactive paginating reader and short enough that a crashed reader does not pin storage for long. This is the ONE lifecycle-timing constant; GC grace and the reclaim horizon are derived const fns of it (catalog::GC_GRACE_MS/GC_HORIZON_MS), never registered separately, so grace<lease can never arise and orphan a live reader.

constants.name: MERGE_TIER_FANOUT
constants.rationale: docs/MERGE-CONTRACT.md#2 — parts per size tier before it is merged. Too low merges constantly (write amplification); too high lets part count drift up (read amplification) before a merge fires. 4 is the boring middle: a tier holds at most 3 parts in steady state, so part count per partition is bounded by ~3xtiers. The soak gate binds it — an unbounded part count fails it.

constants.name: MERGE_TIER_RATIO
constants.rationale: docs/MERGE-CONTRACT.md#2 — the row-count ratio between adjacent size tiers. Equal to the fan-out on purpose: merging a full tier of FANOUT parts produces one part that lands exactly one tier up, so a merged part does not immediately re-trigger its new tier.

constants.name: MERGE_TIER_BASE_ROWS
constants.rationale: docs/MERGE-CONTRACT.md#2 — the upper row count of tier 0, where freshly-ingested batches land, so a batch lands in tier 0 and climbs from there. Near a typical ingest batch size.

constants.name: MERGE_IO_BUDGET_ROWS
constants.rationale: docs/MERGE-CONTRACT.md#2 — the most rows a single merge cycle will move. A cycle admits ops until this is spent, then defers the rest by name, so one cycle can never rewrite the whole store even under an ingest burst. Generous enough that steady-state merges are never starved.

constants.name: MERGE_MAX_OPS
constants.rationale: docs/MERGE-CONTRACT.md#2 — the most merges a single cycle runs (concurrency budget). Bounds how many partitions a cycle touches, so no cycle's work is unbounded even on a store with thousands of partitions. Excess ops are deferred, not dropped.

constants.name: OBJECT_REQUEST_COST_MICROS
constants.rationale: docs/STORAGE-CONTRACT.md#5 — estimated cost of one cold-tier object request, micro-units. BACKEND-CONDITIONAL: a fixed per-call cost (round trip, auth, request charge) independent of size. This is the local-object-store figure (a syscall's worth); an S3-over-WAN request is orders of magnitude more, and a receipt measured against one backend is not the number for the other. Named so EXPLAIN's per-query cost is a consistent unit, re-derived per backend.

constants.name: RETRIEVED_BYTE_COST_PICOS
constants.rationale: docs/STORAGE-CONTRACT.md#5 — estimated cost of retrieving one cold-tier byte, pico-units. BACKEND-CONDITIONAL: per-byte egress/transfer. Local object storage is near-free per byte; S3-over-WAN egress is the dominant term of a real bill. Pico unit so a megabyte is a meaningful integer and the two backends' figures stay distinguishable.

constants.name: CACHE_QUOTA_BYTES
constants.rationale: docs/STORAGE-CONTRACT.md#4 — the local NVMe block cache byte quota. The cache is a bounded, disposable optimization: sized, not unbounded, so it cannot itself become the thing that fills the disk. A full cache evicts under pressure and at the hard limit reports S10 OutOfSpace backpressure rather than growing without bound. 256 MiB is a working default; the real size is a deployment knob. Backend-conditional numbers ride on it.

constants.name: COLD_FETCH_MAX_RETRIES
constants.rationale: docs/STORAGE-CONTRACT.md#4 — bounded retries of a transient cold-tier fetch (5xx, dropped connection) before the query fails with the named remote condition. BACKEND-CONDITIONAL: a remote read hiccups; a small bounded retry turns a transient blip into the correct answer, and the bound turns a dead remote into a NAMED failure not an unbounded hang. Three rides out a blip and names a genuinely-down remote quickly. A truncated/corrupt read is NOT retried — named byte error at once.

constants.name: MULTIPART_THRESHOLD_BYTES
constants.rationale: docs/STORAGE-CONTRACT.md#2 — object size at/above which an upload uses multipart instead of a single PUT, AND the size of each part. BACKEND-CONDITIONAL: a single PUT is fine to the backend's limit, but a large object wants multipart so a mid-upload crash leaves resumable server-side parts that GC's list-and-abort sweep reclaims, rather than re-sending whole. 16 MiB (> S3's 5 MiB minimum part size) is a common part size; a cold-tier rerank.vec crosses it at scale. Multipart initiate/part/complete/abort + the GC sweep are implemented (S11 boundary d); this constant is both the threshold and the part size.
